insights: Fred Luddy handles 77.0% of all 500 incidents—24 times more than the least-assigned agent—indicating a severe, systemic assignment imbalance that risks burnout, error accumulation, and service continuity failure.

The imbalance persists uniformly across all assignment groups (57–88% share for Fred), categories (70–83%), and priority levels (72–78%), confirming it is driven by flawed routing logic—not agent specialization, group constraints, or incident severity.
All four underloaded agents (Beth, Luke, Charlie, Howard) already operate across all five major assignment groups, enabling immediate redistribution without retraining or role redesign.

The Network assignment group presents the highest-impact redistribution opportunity: shifting just 62 of Fred’s 233 Network incidents (26.6%) to underloaded agents would reduce his overall share from 77.0% to ~64.6%, while bringing Beth Anglin—the most capable redistribution target with 47 incidents and full group coverage—closer to workload parity.

Beth Anglin is the optimal first candidate for redistribution: she holds only 9.4% of incidents but has demonstrated cross-group capability in all five assignment groups, offering the largest safe capacity buffer among underloaded agents.

Luke, Charlie, and Howard collectively handle just 13.6% of incidents despite shared group coverage with Fred, indicating substantial latent capacity that can absorb >100 incidents without exceeding current per-agent averages.
summary: The incident assignment system exhibits a critical, systemic imbalance: Fred Luddy carries 77.0% of all incidents—a 24× disparity versus the least-assigned agent—while uniform overrepresentation across every assignment group, category, and priority level confirms this is a routing failure, not a structural or competency issue. All underloaded agents already possess cross-group operational experience, eliminating training barriers to redistribution. The Network group alone offers the largest near-term opportunity, with 62 incidents safely assignable from Fred to others; prioritizing Beth Anglin—currently at 9.4% share but fully qualified across all groups—enables rapid, low-risk rebalancing. Immediate focus should be on adjusting routing rules to enforce proportional distribution and piloting targeted incident shifts to Beth and the other three underutilized agents to validate capacity and refine allocation logic.
